content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


February 4, 2002


Stephen J. Kaufman
Smith Kaufman, LLP
725 So. Figueroa Street, Suite 3200
Los Angeles, CA 90017-5432

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-303

Dear Mr. Kaufman:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the California Climate Action Registry regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice on or an opinion based regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request letter.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTION

Is the California Climate Action Registry required to adopt a conflict of interest code?

CONCLUSION

Yes, the California Climate Action Registry is a state agency under the Act, and is accordingly, required to adopt a conflict of interest code.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; § 83114.)

File No. A 01-303
Page No. 2

FACTS

The California Climate Action Registry ("Registry") is a public-private
partnership on climate change in California that was incorporated earlier this year as a
non-profit public benefit corporation. The Registry will administer a voluntary program
to record and register voluntary greenhouse gas emission reductions and to promote
reductions in greenhouse gas emissions. Greenhouse gas emissions are emissions
released into the atmosphere that contribute to global warming.

The Registry was established as a non-profit corporation by the Secretary of the
Resources Agency pursuant to state legislation and has a seven member board of
directors composed of five public member gubernatorial appointees, the Secretary of the
Resources Agency and the Secretary for Environmental Protection. (Health & Safety
Code § 42821.) The board of directors has met just once as a body.

In September of this year, state legislation established additional specificity to the
responsibilities of the Registry. (SB 527, Chapter 769 Statutes of 2001.) As a result, the
Registry is now legislatively charged with the following responsibilities:

-establish emissions reduction goals;
-design and implement plans to improve energy efficiency and/or utilize
renewable energy capable of achieving emission reduction targets;
-maintain a list of technical assistance and advice providers in coordination with
the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission (more
commonly referred to as the California Energy Commission);
-provide participants with referrals to approved providers for technical assistance
and advice regarding designing programs to establish greenhouse gas emissions
baselines and to monitor, estimate, calculate, report and certify greenhouse gas
emissions;
-adopt procedures and protocols for certification of voluntarily reported baseline
emissions and emissions results to facilitate their recognition in any future
regulatory regime; and
-qualify third-party organizations to certify reported baseline emissions and
emission results.

Initial funding for the Registry has been provided by grants received from the
California Energy Commission and the Energy Foundation. The California Energy
Commission provided a two-year grant totaling $1,000,000. Once the organization
begins its operations, additional funding will be generated from participation fees paid by
entities that participate in the Registry, seminar fees and payments for reports produced
by the Registry. The Registry budget is expected to be $1,650,000 for the first two years.
In its third year, the Registry's funding is supposed to be completely generated by
participation fees.

Private entities can choose to participate in the Registry by submitting
information about their emissions, receiving access to information about emissions
standards and contributing to the development and adoption of standards.

File No. A 01-303
Page No. 3

The Secretary of Resources was statutorily mandated to establish the Registry.
However, rather than establish the Registry as an entity within Resources, the legislation
requires the Registry to be created and run as a non-profit organization.

The Registry expects to receive governmental funds only in its first two years of
operation. The California Energy Commission provided a grant for start up funds of
$600,000 to be used in the Registry's first year, and $400,000 to be used in the Registry's
second year. A grant from the private Energy Foundation, as well as participation fees,
comprises the rest of the Registry's funding in its first two years. After its initial two
years of operation, the Registry will be funded by participation fees, seminar fees,
publication sales and grants from private foundations similar to its grant from the Energy
Foundation. The Registry is not part of the state budget and is not funded through any
state appropriations.

You state that no other state agency in California is charged with responsibilities
similar to those of the Registry-recording and registering voluntary greenhouse gas
reductions -nor has this function been one that has been traditionally performed by a state
agency. The Registry was set up as a non-profit public benefit corporation in order to
make sure the voluntary reporting of participants would not take place within a
governmental regulatory agency. By forming the Registry as a non-profit corporation, the
business, government, and environmental communities have an opportunity to work
together in a partnership towards a common goal of reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

The Registry and its functions do complement existing governmental efforts and
environmental leadership to increase energy efficiency and reduce air pollution by
specifically targeting greenhouse gas emissions. The procedures and protocols to be
adopted by the Registry are the only ones that will be recognized by the state. The
legislation that created the Registry contemplates that greenhouse gas emissions baselines
established under the program will receive consideration under any future governmental
regulatory schemes. Beyond that, however, the Registry's involvement is not
contemplated. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the U.S. Information
Agency each administer a voluntary greenhouse gas emissions monitoring program.
Other states are also in the process of developing voluntary Registry programs. These are
fairly recent innovations that are not properly characterized as traditional governmental
programs at this point in time.

You believe that the board of directors for the Registry is subject to the provisions
of the Bagley-Keene Open Meetings Act. You are not aware of any other similar
statutory provisions to which the Registry is subject. The Registry is recognized as a
California public benefit corporation and has been granted tax-exempt status by the
Internal Revenue Service.

The primary clientele of the Registry will be private businesses and industries that
generate greenhouse gas emissions. Unlike a typical governmental agency, the Registry
has no authority to compel involvement by private entities or to exert its jurisdiction over
them. Private entities can choose to participate in the monitoring and registering of their

File No. A 01-303
Page No. 4

greenhouse gas emissions. They will pay to participate in the Registry, as they would pay
for services provided to them in other business contexts.

In the short-term, the Registry will register greenhouse gas emissions by entities
that generate greenhouse gas emissions within California. These entities are encouraged
to register their emissions generated throughout the United States, not just in California.
Since the Registry is directed to receive information from jurisdictions outside of
California, its functions are not properly characterized as those of a state agency.

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 requires that every agency "adopt and promulgate" a conflict of
interest code. The term "state agency" is defined at § 82049 as follows:

"'State agency' means every state office, department, division, bureau, board and
commission, and the Legislature." (§ 82049.)

The threshold issue is whether the Registry is a state agency and thus, required to
comply with the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. Where a state entity, such as
the Registry, is not definitively included or excluded from coverage under the Act, the
Commission applies the analytical framework set forth in its opinion in In re Siegel
(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62, to assist in making that determination. (See Maas Advice Letter,
No. A-98-261.) However, the framework set forth in Siegel is not a litmus test, and
ultimately the issue must be decided on a case by case basis. (In re Vonk (1981) 6 FPPC
Ops. 1.)

Under Siegel, to determine the nature of a given entity, four criteria are examined:

1. Whether the impetus for formation of the corporation originated with a governmental
agency.

2. Whether it is substantially funded by, or its primary source of funds is, a
governmental agency.

3. Whether one of the principal purposes for which it is formed is to provide services or
undertake obligations which public agencies are legally authorized to perform and
which, in fact, they traditionally have performed; and

4. Whether the corporation is treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions.

The Impetus for Formation of the Registry

From a review of your letter and SB 527 itself, it is clear that the state Legislature
through a mandate to the State Resources Secretary is providing the impetus for
formation of the Registry to act in coordination with the State Air Resources Board and
the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission to develop
baseline standards for "greenhouse" emission levels of participating California

File No. A 01-303
Page No. 5

businesses. The Registry will be involved in developing a certification program for
participating businesses and industries. Therefore, the Registry meets this first criterion.

The Primary Source of Funding for the Registry

Under the facts that you have provided, the state will provide primary start-up
funding for the Registry with supplemental private funding. While it is anticipated that
the Registry will be funded thereafter from participant fees, that is not presently the case.
Therefore, the Registry meets this second criterion.

The Principal Purpose of the Organization

As set forth in your letter and SB 527 itself, the purpose of the Registry is among
other things to develop the above discussed baseline standards and certification process
for "greenhouse" gas emissions. In this regard, the legislation contemplates close
cooperation between the Registry and other state agencies to accomplish these goals. The
State Energy Conservation and Development Commission will work very closely with
the Registry in implementing specific provisions of the legislation. (Health & Safety
Code § 42870.) As such, while it may represent an "innovative" approach, it will be
working very closely with existing state agencies and will be performing traditional
governmental functions in accomplishing the goals of the legislation.

Treatment of the Registry as a Public Agency

While the Registry is a nonprofit corporation, it is established and operated under
a comprehensive scheme of state statutes and regulations under which other state
agencies will play a very significant role. The seven-member board of directors for the
Registry includes five gubernatorial appointees, as well as the Secretary of the Resources
Agency and the Secretary for Environmental Protection. The meetings of the Registry
will, you believe, be subject to the Bagley-Keene Open Meetings Act. (Government Code
§ 11120, et seq.) Your belief is consistent with the public participation provisions of the
legislation. (Health & Safety Code § 42821(c).) Based upon all of these factors, this
fourth criterion is met.

In sum, the Registry meets all of the criteria under the Siegel test. It has all of the
hallmarks of a state agency created to accomplish legislatively mandated goals for a
public purpose in the prevention of pollution and conservation resources. Therefore, it is
a "state agency" within the meaning of the Act and is required to adopt a conflict of
interest code.

You should contact our Division of Technical Assistance for help regarding the
development and adoption of a code.

If you believe an exemption applies, you should follow the procedures set forth in
Regulation 18751 (copy enclosed).

File No. A 01-303
Page No. 6

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: William L. Williams, Jr.
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
